children.title: Neuro-Ophthalmic Anatomy
Bony Anatomy
Skull base foramina: optic canal (CN II + ophthalmic a.), superior orbital fissure (CN III/IV/V1/VI + superior ophthalmic v.), inferior orbital fissure (V2, infraorbital a.)
Orbital apex: convergence of all structures entering orbit; apex syndrome = all cranial nerves affected
Cavernous sinus: CN III, IV, V1, V2 in lateral wall; CN VI medial (most vulnerable); contains ICA
Vascular Anatomy
Anterior Circulation (ICA)
ICA → ophthalmic artery (1st intracranial branch) → central retinal artery + posterior ciliary arteries
Ophthalmic artery supplies optic nerve, retina, choroid, orbit
ICA → ACA + MCA; ACoA connects bilateral ACAs
PCoA connects ICA to PCA (posterior circulation); aneurysm here → CN III palsy
Posterior Circulation (Vertebrobasilar)
Vertebral arteries → basilar artery → PCAs
PICA (posterior inferior cerebellar a.) → lateral medulla; AICA → lateral pons; SCA → cerebellum
PCAs supply occipital cortex (V1); bilateral PCA stroke → cortical blindness
Basilar tip aneurysm → top-of-the-basilar syndrome (CN III palsy + somnolence)
Venous System
Superior ophthalmic vein → cavernous sinus → petrosal sinuses → jugular vein
Cavernous sinus thrombosis: proptosis + chemosis + CN palsies + fever
Cerebral venous thrombosis: ICP elevation + headache ± vision loss; treat with anticoagulation
Afferent Visual Pathways
Retinal ganglion cells → NFL → optic nerve → chiasm → optic tract → LGN → optic radiations → V1 (calcarine cortex)
Chiasm: nasal fibers cross (representing temporal visual fields); temporal fibers stay ipsilateral
Meyer loop (temporal lobe radiations): inferior visual field; 'pie in the sky' defect with temporal lesion
Parietal radiations: superior visual field; 'pie on the floor' defect with parietal lesion
V1 (striate cortex): primary visual; V2/V3/V4/V5: secondary; dorsal stream (where) vs ventral stream (what)
Efferent Visual System
Ocular Motor Cranial Nerves
CN III nucleus (midbrain): SR subnucleus crosses; levator subnucleus is midline/single
CN III fascicle passes through red nucleus + cerebral peduncle; exits interpeduncular fossa
CN IV: only CN to exit dorsally; longest intracranial course; decussates in anterior medullary velum
CN VI nucleus (pons): contains CN VII genu (fascicle wraps around); PPRF nearby
MLF (medial longitudinal fasciculus): connects CN III/IV/VI nuclei + vestibular nuclei; internuclear pathway
Supranuclear Control
Frontal eye fields (FEF, area 8): voluntary contralateral saccades
PPRF (paramedian pontine reticular formation): horizontal gaze center (ipsilateral)
riMLF (rostral interstitial MLF, midbrain): vertical gaze center
Cerebellum: smooth pursuit, VOR calibration, gaze-holding
Superior colliculus: reflexive saccades
Ocular Autonomic Pathways
Sympathetic (3-neuron arc)
1st order: hypothalamus → ipsilateral ciliospinal center of Budge (C8-T2)
2nd order: over lung apex → around subclavian/carotid arteries → superior cervical ganglion
3rd order: along ICA → cavernous sinus → ophthalmic division → dilator muscle + Müller muscle + lower lid retractor
Horner syndrome: ptosis (2mm) + miosis + anhidrosis (1st/2nd order) + lower lid elevation
Pharmacologic testing: cocaine (blocks reuptake) → confirms Horner; hydroxyamphetamine → localizes 3rd order
Parasympathetic
Edinger-Westphal nucleus → CN III → ciliary ganglion → short ciliary nerves → sphincter + ciliary muscle
Light reflex: retina → pretectal nucleus → bilateral EW → CN III → constriction
Near reflex triad: convergence + accommodation + miosis
Sensory & Facial Motor Anatomy
CN V (trigeminal): V1 (ophthalmic) → corneal sensation, forehead; V2 (maxillary) → cheek; V3 (mandibular) → jaw + motor
Corneal reflex: V1 afferent → bilateral CN VII efferent (blink)
CN VII (facial): motor (upper face bilateral cortical input; lower face contralateral only); taste anterior 2/3 tongue; lacrimation
Upper motor neuron CN VII lesion: spares forehead (bilateral cortical input); Lower motor neuron: entire face affected (Bell palsy)
Imaging in Neuro-Ophthalmology
CT
Best for: bone, acute hemorrhage (hyperdense), calcifications, foreign bodies, trauma
Fast; widely available; use in acute settings
CT angiography (CTA): rapid, high-res; detects aneurysms ≥3mm, arterial dissection, AVM
MRI
T1: fat/gadolinium bright; anatomy; T2/FLAIR: water/edema bright; demyelination, ischemia
DWI (diffusion-weighted): acute infarction (bright within minutes-hours)
FLAIR: suppresses CSF; ideal for periventricular MS plaques, cortical lesions
Fat-suppressed T1+Gad: optic nerve enhancement (neuritis), meningeal disease
MRA (MR angiography): non-invasive; detects large vessel aneurysms, stenosis
MRV (MR venography): cerebral venous thrombosis
When & What to Order
Optic neuritis: MRI brain + orbits with fat-suppressed Gad (ON enhancement + MS plaques)
CN III palsy: MRA or CTA urgently to r/o PCoA aneurysm (especially if pupil-involving)
Proptosis/orbital disease: CT first (bone), then MRI if soft tissue/ON detail needed
Papilledema/ICP: MRI brain + MRV (r/o venous thrombosis) + LP
Suspected stroke/TIA: MRI DWI + MRA
Cavernous sinus syndrome: MRI with Gad
Other Modalities
Catheter angiography (DSA): gold standard for aneurysms and AVM; allows intervention
B-scan ultrasound: optic nerve sheath diameter >5mm → elevated ICP; also drusen, masses
OCT (optic coherence tomography): NFL thickness (glaucoma/ON atrophy); GCL loss; disc swelling quantification
OCT-A (angiography): flow-based; macular capillary dropout in ischemia
VEP (visual evoked potential): delayed P100 → demyelination (optic neuritis)
Key Neuroimaging Terms
Hyperdense (CT): acute blood, calcification, dense vessel sign (MCA thrombosis)
Hyperintense T2/FLAIR: edema, demyelination, infarction, tumor
Enhancement (Gad): breakdown of blood-brain barrier; active inflammation, tumor, abscess
Restricted diffusion (DWI bright + ADC dark): acute cytotoxic edema = ischemia
Decreased Vision: Evaluation
History
Monocular vs binocular: monocular = pre-chiasmal; binocular = chiasmal or post-chiasmal
Acute vs subacute vs chronic; painful vs painless
Associated: headache, diplopia, proptosis, jaw claudication, preceding amaurosis fugax, neurologic Sx
Cognitive/behavioral change: suggests cortical/higher visual pathway
Examination
Visual acuity: Snellen; pinhole improves refractive errors but NOT optic nerve/retinal disease
Color vision (Ishihara/HRR): disproportionate loss in optic nerve disease vs retinal disease
Pupil (RAPD): swinging flashlight test; RAPD = afferent defect; absent in bilateral symmetric disease
Fundus: disc swelling (papilledema, NAION), pallor (atrophy), NFL defects, retinal changes
Visual fields: confrontation (screening); automated perimetry (Humphrey 24-2 or 30-2) for threshold
Visual Field Patterns
Central scotoma: macular or optic nerve (especially demyelination)
Arcuate/altitudinal defect: NAION, glaucoma (nerve fiber bundle)
Bitemporal hemianopia: chiasmal compression (pituitary)
Homonymous hemianopia: post-chiasmal; complete = occipital; incomplete = tract/radiation
Quadrantanopia: temporal (Meyer loop, superior quadrant) or parietal (inferior quadrant)
Constricted field: glaucoma, RP, papilledema (chronic)
Adjunct Testing
OCT: NFL thinning (chronic ON disease), RNFL thickening (acute disc swelling)
FAF (fundus autofluorescence): RPE lipofuscin; hyper = active disease; hypo = atrophy
FFA/ICGA: vascular disease, CNV, ischemia
ERG: photoreceptor/retinal function; full-field vs multifocal (macular)
VEP: optic nerve conduction; delayed P100 in demyelination
Photostress recovery test: prolonged recovery → macular disease (not optic nerve)
Contrast sensitivity: reduced in ON disease, amblyopia
Decreased Vision: Retinal, ON & Chiasmal Disease
Optic Neuropathies
Optic Neuritis
Typical: young woman; painful (retrobulbar); acute unilateral vision loss; RAPD; disc normal (retrobulbar) or swollen (papillitis)
Strongly associated with MS; MRI brain: >2 T2 lesions → high risk of MS conversion
ONTT: IV steroids speed recovery but don't change final VA; oral prednisone alone → ↑relapse risk
Atypical features (bilateral, painless, severe, no recovery, older patient) → r/o NMOSD, MOGAD, sarcoid, syphilis
NAION (Non-Arteritic Anterior Ischemic ON)
Most common acute optic neuropathy >50 years; painless; altitudinal visual field loss (inferior > superior)
Crowded disc ('disc at risk'): small cup-to-disc ratio
Risk factors: hypertension, DM, hyperlipidemia, OSA, nocturnal hypotension, PDE5 inhibitors
No proven treatment; fellow eye risk ~15-20% lifetime; aspirin does not prevent recurrence
AAION (Arteritic - Giant Cell Arteritis)
Age >50 (usually >70); jaw claudication, scalp tenderness, temporal headache, elevated ESR/CRP/platelets
Chalky white disc edema; fellow eye involvement risk within days without treatment
Diagnosis: temporal artery biopsy (skip lesions; do within 2 weeks of starting steroids)
Treatment: immediate high-dose IV methylprednisolone → oral prednisone taper; do NOT wait for biopsy
Papilledema
Bilateral disc swelling due to ↑ICP; transient visual obscurations; preserved VA early
Causes: IIH, intracranial mass, venous sinus thrombosis, meningitis, hydrocephalus
Frisen grading: grade 1-5 (C-shaped halo to severe with hemorrhages)
IIH: obese young woman; LP opening pressure >25cmH₂O; normal MRI (empty sella, bilateral transverse sinus stenosis); treat with acetazolamide ± weight loss ± CSF diversion
Compressive Optic Neuropathy
Slow progressive vision loss; color desaturation; central scotoma; optic atrophy late
Causes: meningioma, pituitary adenoma, thyroid eye disease (orbital apex), aneurysm
Optociliary shunt vessels: chronic compression (meningioma of ON sheath)
Leber Hereditary Optic Neuropathy (LHON)
Mitochondrial (maternal); young males predominantly; bilateral sequential painless central vision loss
mt-ND1/4/6 mutations (11778 most common); peripapillary telangiectasias on exam
Treatment: idebenone; avoid smoking/alcohol (mitochondrial stressors)
Other Optic Neuropathies
Toxic/nutritional: B12 deficiency, folate, tobacco-alcohol amblyopia, ethambutol, methanol, amiodarone → bilateral centrocecal scotomas
Traumatic: direct (laceration) or indirect (closed head trauma with canalicular fracture); no proven treatment
Infiltrative: lymphoma, leukemia, sarcoid, carcinomatous meningitis
Radiation optic neuropathy: delayed (months-years); painless; severe; bevacizumab may help
Chiasmal Disorders
Classic defect: bitemporal hemianopia (nasal fibers cross at chiasm)
Junction scotoma: ipsilateral central + contralateral superior temporal (Wilbrand knee)
Pituitary adenoma: most common cause; compresses chiasm from below; visual field recovery after surgery correlates with preop NFL thickness on OCT
Other causes: meningioma, craniopharyngioma, glioma, aneurysm, sarcoid, arachnoid cyst
Retinal Causes
Autoimmune/paraneoplastic retinopathy (CAR, MAR): antibodies against retinal antigens; assoc. with small cell lung ca (CAR), melanoma (MAR)
Cone dystrophy: central vision + color loss; photoaversion; ERG: reduced cone response
AZOOR/AIBSE/MEWDS: white dot syndromes; unilateral; young women; visual field loss + photopsias; usually self-limited
Retrochiasmal Visual Dysfunction
Optic Tract
Incongruous homonymous hemianopia (most incongruous of all post-chiasmal lesions)
Contralateral RAPD (more temporal fibers than nasal cross)
Bow-tie (band) atrophy of optic disc in ipsilateral eye
Lateral Geniculate Nucleus (LGN)
Rare; dual vascular supply → wedge-shaped sectoral defects sparing horizontal meridian
Anterior choroidal artery (AChA) vs lateral posterior choroidal artery lesions produce distinct field patterns
Optic Radiations
Temporal lobe (Meyer loop): superior homonymous quadrantanopia ('pie in the sky')
Parietal lobe: inferior homonymous quadrantanopia ('pie on the floor') ± visual neglect, optokinetic nystagmus asymmetry
Occipital lobe (V1): congruous homonymous hemianopia, often with macular sparing
Cortical Visual Impairment
Bilateral occipital damage: cortical blindness; normal pupil reactions (midbrain intact)
Anton syndrome: cortical blindness + denial of visual loss (confabulation)
Balint syndrome: simultanagnosia + optic ataxia + ocular apraxia (bilateral parieto-occipital)
Causes: bilateral PCA infarcts, hypoxia, PRES, trauma
Transient Visual Loss
Monocular Transient Visual Loss (TMVL)
Amaurosis fugax: embolic (carotid plaque, cardiac, hypercoagulable); positive scotoma; brief (seconds-minutes)
Ocular causes: transient IOP elevation, angle closure, hyphema, vitreous hemorrhage
Vascular: central retinal artery spasm, hypoperfusion (orthostatic), vasospasm (Raynaud-like, migraine)
Orbital: orbital compartment syndrome (positional), vascular compression
Workup: carotid Doppler/CTA, cardiac echo, lipids, CBC, coags, hypercoagulable panel
Binocular Transient Visual Loss
Migraine with aura: scintillating scotoma (positive); gradual spread over 20-30 min; followed by headache; binocular
Vertebrobasilar TIA: bilateral vision loss + other posterior circulation Sx (vertigo, diplopia, ataxia)
ICP-related: transient obscurations in papilledema; seconds; positional
Occipital seizure: brief stereotyped colored visual phenomena
Illusions, Hallucinations & Higher Cortical Function
Hallucinations
Charles Bonnet syndrome: formed hallucinations in visually impaired patients; normal cognition; no psychiatric disease
Peduncular hallucinosis: vivid formed hallucinations; midbrain/thalamic lesion
Migraine aura: unformed (fortification spectra, scintillations); unilateral or bilateral
Seizure (occipital): simple colored blobs/circles; brief, stereotyped
Drug-induced, psychiatric (distinguish from neurologic)
Disorders of Higher Cortical Function
Prosopagnosia: inability to recognize faces; bilateral fusiform gyrus (ventral stream)
Visual agnosia: cannot recognize objects despite intact vision; ventral stream lesion
Alexia without agraphia: can write but not read; left occipital + splenium of corpus callosum
Simultanagnosia (Balint): cannot see multiple objects at once; bilateral parieto-occipital
Neglect/hemi-inattention: right parietal lesion → left-sided visual neglect
Anosognosia/Anton syndrome: denial of blindness
Abnormal Ocular Motility & Diplopia
Approach to Diplopia
Monocular (persists with fellow eye covered): ocular cause (irregular cornea, lens, retina)
Binocular (resolves with cover): misalignment; classify by direction and gaze
Comitant (deviation same in all gazes): strabismus, decompensated phoria
Incomitant (varies with gaze): paretic or restrictive
Paretic (forced duction negative) vs restrictive (forced duction positive)
CN III Palsy
Clinical: ptosis (complete) + down-and-out eye + mydriasis (if compressive)
Pupil-involving → compressive until proven otherwise → urgent CTA/MRA → r/o PCoA aneurysm
Pupil-sparing + complete = microvascular (DM, HTN); usually recovers in 3 months
Aberrant regeneration: lid elevation on adduction/downgaze; never in microvascular → r/o mass
Fascicular: with contralateral tremor (Benedict) or hemiplegia (Weber)
CN IV Palsy
Most common cause of isolated vertical diplopia; vertical/torsional
Parks-Bielschowsky 3-step test: identifies paretic muscle
Worse looking down + away; head tilt contralateral; excyclotorsion on double Maddox rod
Cause: trauma (bilateral CN IV), congenital (decompensated), microvascular, mass
CN VI Palsy
Esotropia + horizontal diplopia worse at distance looking ipsilaterally
Isolated CN VI palsy in adult: microvascular first; if young or persistent → MRI (pontine lesion, ICP elevation, tumor)
Bilateral CN VI: ↑ICP (false localizing sign), trauma, meningitis
Gradenigo syndrome: CN VI + ipsilateral facial pain (petrous apex infection/tumor)
Internuclear Ophthalmoplegia (INO)
MLF lesion: ipsilateral adduction deficit + contralateral abducting nystagmus
Young bilateral INO: MS until proven otherwise
Older unilateral INO: brainstem infarct
One-and-a-half syndrome: ipsilateral PPRF/CN VI nucleus + MLF → ipsilateral gaze palsy + INO; only abduction of contralateral eye preserved
Cavernous Sinus & Orbital Apex Syndromes
Cavernous sinus: CN III + IV + V1 + V2 + VI ± Horner; causes: aneurysm, thrombosis, tumor, fistula, meningioma, pituitary apoplexy
Orbital apex: CN III + IV + VI + V1 + optic nerve (vision loss distinguishes from CS syndrome)
Tolosa-Hunt syndrome: painful ophthalmoplegia; granulomatous; steroid-responsive; diagnosis of exclusion
Restrictive & Myopathic Causes
Thyroid eye disease: IR most commonly affected → restriction in upgaze; forced duction positive
Orbital blow-out fracture: IR or IO entrapment → restriction
CPEO: bilateral ptosis + ophthalmoplegia; mitochondrial; Kearns-Sayre if <20yo + pigmentary retinopathy + heart block
Brown syndrome: inability to elevate in adduction (SO tendon sheath restriction or trochlea)
Neuromuscular Junction
Myasthenia gravis: fatiguable ptosis + ophthalmoplegia; variable; Cogan lid twitch sign
Ice test: improves ptosis in MG (cold inhibits AChE); useful bedside test
AChR Ab (85% generalized, 50% ocular MG); anti-MuSK Ab; edrophonium test
Lambert-Eaton: proximal weakness improving with repetition; lung cancer assoc.
Supranuclear Ocular Motility Disorders
Eye Movement Systems
Saccades: fast, voluntary gaze shifts; FEF → contralateral PPRF; cerebellar vermis calibrates
Smooth pursuit: follows moving target; ipsilateral parieto-occipital → PPRF; requires intact target
VOR (vestibulo-ocular reflex): stabilizes gaze during head movement; gain = 1 normal
Vergence: convergence (medial recti) + divergence; near reflex triad
Optokinetic nystagmus (OKN): reflexive; asymmetric OKN → parietal lesion (ipsilesional pursuit failure)
Supranuclear Gaze Palsies
Horizontal gaze palsy: PPRF or CN VI nucleus lesion; eyes deviate toward lesion (pontine) or away (hemisphere/FEF)
Dorsal midbrain (Parinaud) syndrome: upgaze palsy + convergence-retraction nystagmus + light-near dissociation + lid retraction (Collier sign); pineal region tumors, hydrocephalus
PSP (progressive supranuclear palsy): downgaze palsy + vertical saccade slowing; Parkinson-plus
Skew deviation: vertical misalignment; otolithic; indicates posterior fossa lesion (vs trochlear palsy)
Nystagmus & Spontaneous Eye Movement Disorders
Terminology
Jerk nystagmus: slow drift + fast corrective saccade; named by fast phase direction
Pendular nystagmus: equal velocity in both directions; acquired = MS, oculopalatal tremor
Alexander's law: jerk nystagmus increases in gaze toward fast phase direction
Early-Onset Nystagmus
Infantile nystagmus syndrome (INS/congenital): horizontal; dampened by convergence; null point; foveation periods present; associated with albinism, aniridia, achromatopsia
Spasmus nutans: nystagmus + head nodding + torticollis; benign and self-resolving; must r/o chiasmal glioma (MRI)
Latent/manifest latent nystagmus: worse when one eye covered; jerk toward viewing eye
Vestibular Nystagmus
Peripheral (labyrinth/CN VIII): horizontal-torsional; inhibited by fixation; unidirectional; BPPV (posterior canal, Dix-Hallpike positive), vestibular neuritis
Central (brainstem/cerebellum): not inhibited by fixation; can be vertical or direction-changing; gaze-evoked; skew deviation
HINTS test (bedside stroke vs neuritis): Head Impulse (normal = central), Nystagmus (direction-changing = central), Test of Skew (present = central) → any central feature → MRI urgently
Specific Nystagmus Types
Gaze-evoked nystagmus: drugs (AEDs, alcohol), cerebellar disease, Wernicke
Downbeat nystagmus: fast phase down; cerebellar (Arnold-Chiari, paraneoplastic, Mg deficiency); treat with 4-aminopyridine or clonazepam
Upbeat nystagmus: medulla or anterior vermis; Wernicke, MS, tumor
See-saw nystagmus: one eye rises+intorts while other falls+extorts; parasellar/chiasmal lesion
Convergence-retraction nystagmus: attempted upgaze triggers convergence saccades; dorsal midbrain (Parinaud)
Acquired pendular nystagmus: MS, oculopalatal tremor; treat with memantine/gabapentin
Saccadic Intrusions
Square wave jerks: small saccades away + back; normal if rare; cerebellar disease if prominent
Opsoclonus: chaotic multidirectional saccades; paraneoplastic (anti-Ri, neuroblastoma in children), encephalitis; treat underlying cause
Superior oblique myokymia: monocular intorsion/vertical oscillopsia; microvascular compression of CN IV; treat with memantine or carbamazepine
Pupillary Abnormalities
Approach to Anisocoria
Step 1: Is anisocoria worse in bright or dim light?
Worse in bright light (larger pupil abnormal): CN III palsy (+ ptosis, ophthalmoplegia), Adie tonic pupil, pharmacologic mydriasis
Worse in dim light (smaller pupil abnormal): Horner syndrome, pharmacologic miosis
Equal in both conditions: physiologic anisocoria (~20% of normal people; <1mm difference)
Horner Syndrome
Ptosis (2mm, upper) + lower lid elevation (upside-down ptosis) + miosis + anhidrosis (1st/2nd order)
Cocaine test: confirms Horner (both pupils dilate normally; Horner pupil does not)
Hydroxyamphetamine test: dilates if 3rd order neuron intact; fails if 3rd order lesion
Apraclonidine test: reverses anisocoria in Horner (denervation supersensitivity); preferred clinically
Causes by order: 1st (hypothalamic stroke, MS, syrinx, tumor); 2nd (lung apex tumor - Pancoast, carotid dissection, aortic aneurysm); 3rd (ICA dissection, cavernous sinus, cluster headache)
New Horner + ipsilateral neck pain = carotid/vertebral dissection until proven otherwise → urgent MRI/MRA
Adie Tonic Pupil
Young women; unilateral large pupil; poor or tonic light reaction; better near response (light-near dissociation)
Sector palsy of iris sphincter on slit lamp; denervation supersensitivity to dilute pilocarpine (0.1%)
Adie syndrome: tonic pupil + absent deep tendon reflexes
Cause: ciliary ganglion damage (viral, ischemic); benign
CN III Palsy (Pupil-Involving)
Dilated, poorly reactive pupil + ophthalmoplegia + ptosis → compressive (aneurysm, uncal herniation)
Pupil-sparing complete CN III → microvascular ischemia (DM/HTN); but beware: pupil may be spared in early compression
Light-Near Dissociation
Poor light response but preserved near response
Causes: Adie tonic pupil, Argyll Robertson pupil (syphilis), dorsal midbrain syndrome, bilateral severe retinal/optic nerve disease, aberrant CN III regeneration
Argyll Robertson: bilateral, small, irregular; accommodation intact; syphilis (neurosyphilis)
Eyelid & Facial Abnormalities
Ptosis
Neurogenic: CN III palsy (+ ophthalmoplegia), Horner (2mm), Marcus Gunn jaw-winking (CN V/III synkinesis)
Myogenic: CPEO, myasthenia gravis (fatigable), myotonic dystrophy
Aponeurotic (involutional): most common acquired; levator dehiscence; high lid crease
Mechanical: heavy lid (tumor, edema, chalazion)
Congenital: poor levator function; risk of amblyopia → treat early
MRD1 (margin reflex distance): normal >3mm; levator function: >8mm normal
Eyelid Retraction
Thyroid eye disease (Dalrymple sign): most common cause; lid lag (von Graefe sign)
Dorsal midbrain syndrome (Collier sign): bilateral lid retraction + upgaze palsy
Contralateral ptosis: ipsilateral lid retraction via Hering's law
CN VII Disorders
Underactivity (Paresis/Palsy)
LMN (Bell palsy): entire ipsilateral face; inability to close eye (Bell phenomenon = upward eye roll) → corneal exposure risk
Bell palsy: most common; HSV-1 reactivation likely; treat with oral steroids ± antivirals within 72h
UMN (stroke, tumor): forehead sparing (bilateral cortical representation of upper face)
Other LMN causes: Ramsay Hunt (VZV, ear vesicles), parotid tumor, sarcoid, Lyme, Melkersson-Rosenthal
Corneal protection: lubricants, patching, tarsal surgery (gold weight implant)
Overactivity
Hemifacial spasm: unilateral, starts orbicularis; compression of CN VII by vessel at root entry zone; treat with botulinum toxin or MVD (microvascular decompression)
Blepharospasm: bilateral involuntary orbicularis contractions; functional blindness; treat with botulinum toxin
Synkinesis: aberrant regeneration after Bell palsy; crocodile tears (gustatory lacrimation)
Head, Ocular & Facial Pain
Migraine
Classic: unilateral throbbing + nausea/vomiting + photo/phonophobia; +/- aura (scintillating scotoma, fortification spectra)
Retinal migraine: monocular; rare; diagnosis of exclusion (r/o embolic TMVL)
Hemiplegic migraine: aura + motor symptoms; CACNA1A/ATP1A2/SCN1A mutations in familial form
Treatment: acute (triptans, NSAIDs, antiemetics); preventive (topiramate, propranolol, amitriptyline, CGRP inhibitors)
Trigeminal Autonomic Cephalgias (TACs)
Cluster headache: severe unilateral periorbital + ipsilateral autonomic features (lacrimation, ptosis, nasal congestion); 15-180min; O2 + sumatriptan acute; verapamil preventive
SUNCT/SUNA: very brief (seconds); multiple daily attacks; often trigeminal neuralgia-like
Paroxysmal hemicrania: like cluster but shorter + more frequent; absolutely indomethacin responsive
Hemicrania continua: continuous unilateral + autonomic; indomethacin responsive
Giant Cell Arteritis (GCA)
Age >50 (usually >70); temporal headache + scalp tenderness + jaw claudication + constitutional symptoms
ESR >50, CRP elevated, platelets elevated; TAB: granulomatous inflammation with giant cells
Ocular involvement: AAION (most common) + CRAO + CN palsies + cortical infarction
EMERGENCY: start IV methylprednisolone 1g/day immediately; do NOT wait for biopsy
Trigeminal Neuralgia
Electric shock-like lancinating pain in CN V distribution; triggered by touch, eating, talking
Classic: vascular compression of CN V root; symptomatic: MS (demyelinating plaque at root entry zone)
Treatment: carbamazepine (1st line) → oxcarbazepine → MVD (surgery)
Other Pain Syndromes
Herpes zoster ophthalmicus (HZO): V1 distribution; Hutchinson sign (nose tip involvement) → high risk of ocular involvement; treat with antivirals
ICA dissection: ipsilateral Horner + ipsilateral head/neck pain + pulsatile tinnitus; can cause stroke; anticoagulation vs antiplatelet
Trochleitis: pain/tenderness over trochlea (superonasal orbit); worse with upgaze; treat with local steroid injection
Occipital neuralgia: C2 distribution; occipital nerve block diagnostic + therapeutic
Functional Neurological Symptom Disorder
Previously: conversion disorder; real symptoms, no structural disease; NOT malingering
Clinical clues: tubular visual field (does not expand with distance), spiral visual field, normal VEP, normal ERG, inconsistency between claimed vision and behavior
Positive signs: Hoover sign (hip extension weakness resolves with contralateral hip flexion resistance), give-way weakness
Optokinetic nystagmus (OKN): present in functional blindness (demonstrates intact visual pathway)
Management: non-judgmental explanation; multidisciplinary (neurology + psychiatry + physiotherapy); prognosis better if early diagnosis
Systemic Disease & Neuro-Ophthalmic Manifestations
Multiple Sclerosis
Most common demyelinating disease; relapsing-remitting most common form
Neuro-ophthalmic: optic neuritis (most common presenting symptom), INO (bilateral = pathognomonic for MS), nystagmus, diplopia
Uhthoff phenomenon: vision worsens with heat/exercise (NOT a new attack)
MRI: periventricular T2 lesions (Dawson fingers), juxtacortical, infratentorial, spinal cord lesions
McDonald criteria: dissemination in space + time; OCT NFL thinning as biomarker
Treatment: disease-modifying therapy (interferons, glatiramer, natalizumab, ocrelizumab, alemtuzumab)
NMOSD & MOGAD
NMOSD: AQP4-IgG antibody; severe bilateral ON + transverse myelitis; area postrema syndrome (intractable hiccups/nausea)
MOGAD: MOG-IgG; bilateral ON or disc-prominent ON; better prognosis than NMOSD; periorbital pain; disc swelling common
Both: poor recovery from ON attacks vs MS; NOT treated with standard MS DMTs; use steroids + azathioprine/rituximab/eculizumab (NMOSD)
Myasthenia Gravis
AChR-Ab (85% generalized); ocular MG: ptosis + diplopia only; can mimic any eye movement disorder
Fatigability: worse at end of day; ice test improves ptosis; Cogan lid twitch sign
Diagnosis: AChR-Ab, anti-MuSK (seronegative), edrophonium test, single-fiber EMG (most sensitive)
Thymoma in 15%; thymus hyperplasia in 60% → thymectomy
Treatment: pyridostigmine (symptomatic); steroids + immunosuppression; IVIG/plasma exchange (crisis)
Myasthenic crisis: respiratory failure; ICU; avoid precipitants (aminoglycosides, fluoroquinolones, Mg, beta blockers)
Thyroid Eye Disease
Graves disease; bilateral (often asymmetric); proptosis + EOM enlargement + lid retraction
Restrictive myopathy: IR > MR > SR > LR; forced duction positive
Dysthyroid optic neuropathy: compressive at apex; early treatment with steroids/decompression
CAS (clinical activity score) guides immunosuppression; teprotumumab (anti-IGF-1R) for active disease
Cerebrovascular Disorders
CRAO: painless sudden monocular vision loss; cherry red spot; embolism (carotid, cardiac); workup same as stroke; rare reperfusion window
Vertebrobasilar stroke: diplopia, nystagmus, ataxia, hemianopia; PICA (Wallenberg syndrome = ipsilateral face + contralateral body sensory loss)
Cerebral aneurysm: Berry aneurysm; PCoA most common ophthalmic; sudden onset worst headache (SAH); urgent CTA/LP
Carotid-cavernous fistula: direct (traumatic) or indirect (dural); pulsatile proptosis + chemosis + bruit + elevated IOP; treat with endovascular embolization
AVM: headache, seizure, hemorrhage; bruit; neuroradiology intervention
Cerebral venous thrombosis: papilledema + headache + venous infarcts; anticoagulation
Neurocutaneous Syndromes (Phakomatoses)
NF1 (neurofibromatosis 1): Lisch nodules (iris hamartomas), optic pathway glioma, plexiform neurofibroma; café-au-lait spots
NF2: bilateral acoustic neuromas; posterior subcapsular cataract; meningiomas; epiretinal membranes
Tuberous sclerosis (TSC1/TSC2): retinal astrocytic hamartoma ('giant drüsen'), depigmented skin patches, seizures, cortical tubers
Sturge-Weber: port-wine stain (V1) + ipsilateral glaucoma + leptomeningeal angioma + choroidal hemangioma
von Hippel-Lindau: retinal hemangioblastoma + CNS hemangioblastoma + clear cell renal cell carcinoma; VHL gene
Selected Infectious Diseases
HIV: CMV retinitis (CD4 <50; 'pizza pie' hemorrhagic necrosis); treat with ganciclovir/valganciclovir
Herpes (HSV/VZV): ARN (acute retinal necrosis), ON, encephalitis; treat with IV acyclovir
Syphilis: 'the great imitator'; CN palsies, ON, uveitis, Argyll Robertson pupils; treat with IV penicillin G
Lyme: CN VII palsy most common ocular manifestation; also ON, uveitis; treat with doxycycline/ceftriaxone
Toxoplasmosis: retinochoroiditis; 'headlight in the fog' appearance; immunocompromised at risk for CNS involvement
Tuberculosis: granulomatous ON, choroiditis, cranial neuropathies (CN VI most common in meningitis)
Top 30 Exam Facts
Optic neuritis: young woman + painful VA loss + RAPD → MS association; IV steroids speed recovery only
AAION (GCA): immediate IV steroids; do NOT wait for biopsy; jaw claudication + ESR/CRP elevated
NAION: painless altitudinal loss; crowded disc; microvascular risk factors; no proven treatment
IIH: obese young woman; bilateral papilledema; normal MRI; ↑LP opening pressure; acetazolamide
Parinaud syndrome: upgaze palsy + convergence-retraction nystagmus + light-near dissociation + lid retraction; dorsal midbrain/pineal
Bitemporal hemianopia → chiasmal compression (pituitary adenoma most common)
CN III palsy + pupil involvement → PCoA aneurysm until proven otherwise; urgent CTA/MRA
CN III palsy + pupil sparing + complete → microvascular ischemia; observe 3 months
CN III aberrant regeneration → NOT ischemic; always r/o compressive mass
INO (MLF lesion): adduction deficit ipsilateral + abducting nystagmus contralateral; bilateral → MS
One-and-a-half syndrome: ipsilateral horizontal gaze palsy + INO; only contralateral abduction preserved
CN IV palsy: most common cause of isolated vertical diplopia; Parks 3-step test
Horner: ptosis + miosis + anhidrosis; new + neck pain → carotid dissection (urgent MRA)
Adie tonic pupil: large + poor light reaction + better near response; dilute pilocarpine (0.1%) constricts
Argyll Robertson pupils: bilateral small irregular, light-near dissociation → neurosyphilis
HINTS test: normal head impulse + direction-changing nystagmus + skew deviation = CENTRAL cause
Downbeat nystagmus: cerebellar (Arnold-Chiari); treat with 4-aminopyridine
See-saw nystagmus: parasellar/chiasmal lesion
Opsoclonus: paraneoplastic (neuroblastoma in children, lung/breast in adults)
Bell palsy: LMN CN VII; entire face; HSV-1; steroids ± acyclovir within 72h; protect cornea
Hemifacial spasm: CN VII compression at REZ; treat with botulinum toxin or MVD
MG: fatigable ptosis + diplopia; AChR-Ab; ice test; thymoma in 15%; avoid aminoglycosides
NMOSD (AQP4): severe ON + transverse myelitis; worse prognosis than MS; rituximab/eculizumab
MOGAD (MOG-IgG): disc swelling + good recovery; bilateral ON common; better prognosis than NMOSD
Uhthoff phenomenon: vision worsens with heat/exercise in MS; NOT a new attack
Charles Bonnet: formed hallucinations in visually impaired; normal cognition; benign; no treatment needed
Cortical blindness: normal pupils; Anton syndrome = denial of blindness; bilateral PCA infarcts
NF1: Lisch nodules + optic glioma; NF2: bilateral acoustic neuromas + PSC cataract
Von Hippel-Lindau: retinal hemangioblastoma → treat with laser/anti-VEGF; screen for systemic tumors
Functional visual loss: tubular fields + normal VEP/ERG + OKN present + inconsistency = key features
